caveats: a: Occupation data are for NAICS 324, dominated by refining, and are not six-digit-specific.
a: Task exposure is judgmentally mapped and excludes already-automated process control.
rho: No adoption study specific to small U.S. lubricant formulators was found.
rho: The range assumes adequate digital records and excludes autonomous safety-critical control.
e: The fragmentation evidence is global and includes distributors as well as manufacturers.
e: Eligibility is not observed among the injected U.S. firm population.
s5: Cross-industry owner intentions are not completed transactions.
s5: The survey's typical firms are smaller than the $1-10M EBITDA lens.
q: No representative contract sample was available.
q: Retention will differ sharply between proprietary specialty fluids and specification-equivalent commodity lubricants.
d5: The cited forecast is global and vendor-derived within an issuer filing.
d5: Constant-quality quantity must be inferred from market-value and profit-pool forecasts.
o: Operator requirement is inferred from the physical product and compliance workflow, not directly measured.
o: The estimate may overstate persistence where OEM-filled-for-life components eliminate aftermarket demand.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B product sales under customer specifications, purchase orders, distributor relationships, and some supply agreements; revenue is primarily per unit shipped, supported by formulation, testing, reliability, and technical service.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code spans commodity automotive lubricants and technically differentiated industrial formulations. The lens is narrowed to independent specialty B2B formulators because their repeat supply and technical-service model is coherent and distinct from captive or mass-market production.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive refinery units, vertically integrated oil-major operations, commodity-only consumer motor-oil brands, distributors without manufacturing, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market formulators and blenders that repeatedly supply specialty lubricating oils, greases, and related technical support to external industrial, commercial-vehicle, and equipment customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for the broader NAICS 324 petroleum-and-coal-products subsector, not 324191 specialty formulators, and reports headcount rather than task exposure. I map office, commercial, planning, engineering-analysis, documentation, and portions of supervisory/quality work to AI-addressable tasks, while treating blending, material movement, maintenance, and hands-on laboratory work as mostly unexposed. The refinery-heavy parent mix likely understates office and formulation work in the narrowed lens, biasing the estimate downward.
caveats: Occupation data are for NAICS 324, dominated by refining, and are not six-digit-specific.
Task exposure is judgmentally mapped and excludes already-automated process control.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted occupational roster and task diary from 10-15 independent 324191 formulators, linked to O*NET task statements and current automation status, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: BLS shows a production-intensive workforce, including 18,720 refinery/pump operators, 5,230 mixing and blending operators, 4,980 production supervisors, and 3,290 heavy truck drivers in the broader subsector. AI can assist scheduling, purchasing, document control, formulation search, customer service, quoting, and exception analysis, but it cannot directly replace most physical production and movement tasks.

d5.bridge: Moove's filing cites Kline's global real lubricants-market growth of 1.3% annually from 2023-2033 and lubricant-manufacturer profit-pool growth of 1.2%, not constant-quality U.S. quantity for eligible 324191 firms. I translate this into a slower 0.8% annual base quantity path over five years because value growth includes premiumization and global regions grow faster than the United States; electrification could lower automotive volumes while industrial and specialty uses support demand. The global proxy likely biases U.S. quantity growth upward.
caveats: The cited forecast is global and vendor-derived within an issuer filing.
Constant-quality quantity must be inferred from market-value and profit-pool forecasts.
d5.change_evidence: Kline or Freedonia U.S. lubricant demand volumes by automotive, industrial, process oil, and grease segment through 2031, adjusted for product life and quality, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Lubricants remain consumable inputs for installed industrial machinery and vehicle fleets. Premium synthetic adoption and specialized applications support value, but longer drain intervals and electric-vehicle penetration restrain physical volume. The resulting base implies modest real quantity growth rather than the full cited market-value rate.

caveats: The fragmentation evidence is global and includes distributors as well as manufacturers.
Eligibility is not observed among the injected U.S. firm population.
e.change_evidence: A coded census of the 82 injected LMM-band firms using establishment websites, ownership records, product mix, manufacturing assets, and customer type would directly establish eligibility.
e.rationale: The narrowed lens excludes captive plants, oil-major subsidiaries, commodity-only brands, and firms whose economics are primarily distribution. Independent specialty formulators are nevertheless a meaningful segment because the industry is fragmented and product complexity supports specialized regional producers.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 324191 ($1,047,936,613) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 324191 ($19,608,290,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $1,590,936 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 324191); class <5: 47 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,983,170 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $290,534 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,744,714 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $548,601 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,066,031 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,035,174 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 50 firms, avg revenue/firm $38,868,340 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,694,212 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $152,699,565 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $22,370,486 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 35 firms, avg revenue/firm $392,245,971 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $57,464,035 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 82

caveats: Operator requirement is inferred from the physical product and compliance workflow, not directly measured.
The estimate may overstate persistence where OEM-filled-for-life components eliminate aftermarket demand.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement interviews and equipment-OEM data quantifying centralized self-service, lubricant-life extension, and supplier displacement by automated dispensing over five years would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Nearly all year-five lubricant quantity still requires an accountable formulator or manufacturer because formulas must be blended, tested, packaged, documented, and delivered. Software may automate selection, monitoring, and administrative work but does not eliminate physical consumables or product-liability accountability; a small share can shift to self-service standardized supply or be avoided through improved maintenance and longer life.

caveats: No representative contract sample was available.
Retention will differ sharply between proprietary specialty fluids and specification-equivalent commodity lubricants.
q.change_evidence: Customer-level gross-margin bridges, contract clauses, bid histories, renewal price changes, and win/loss records for three years before and after measurable productivity changes would directly inform retained benefit.
q.rationale: Specialty formulations, qualification costs, supply reliability, and technical service create switching friction, allowing part of an implemented cost benefit to remain with the operator. However, product revenue is visible per unit, large customers and distributors negotiate, and raw-material volatility encourages periodic repricing, so savings are likely shared over renewals.

caveats: No adoption study specific to small U.S. lubricant formulators was found.
The range assumes adequate digital records and excludes autonomous safety-critical control.
rho.change_evidence: Site-level before/after studies from lubricant formulators showing production hours, quality escapes, laboratory cycle time, and administrative staffing for AI deployments would materially move implementation expectations.
rho.rationale: Within the exposed task pool, document, commercial, scheduling, and analytic copilots can be adopted within five years, but plant-control and quality decisions require integration, traceable data, human review, and reliability. NIST identifies both AI-enabled quality-control opportunities and persistent integration, data-management, explainability, and reliability barriers in industrial settings.

s5.bridge: Gallup surveys U.S. business owners across industries and sizes, not eligible 324191 firms; 22% of employer-business owners reported plans to sell or transfer within five years. I use 20% as the base after allowing for plans that do not become qualifying control transfers, while the mature manufacturing population could push upward. The net error direction is uncertain.
caveats: Cross-industry owner intentions are not completed transactions.
The survey's typical firms are smaller than the $1-10M EBITDA lens.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, BizBuySell, and private-broker transaction records for U.S. lubricant manufacturers, paired with owner-age and succession interviews for the eligible population, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Employer businesses are substantially more transferable than nonemployers, and 22% reported a five-year sale or transfer plan. Eligible firms have plants, staff, formulas, and customer relationships that can transfer, but family gifts, failed processes, and internal reorganizations do not all qualify.

principal_driver: Recurring physical consumable demand plus formulation and qualification complexity sustains operator-required customer relationships.
principal_weakness: AI-addressable labor is a minority of a production-heavy cost base, and the independent eligible population is not directly measured.

caveats: The labor-exposure estimate maps occupations from refinery-dominated NAICS 324 to a narrowed six-digit specialty-formulator lens without a payroll-weighted task study; the PROXY label and bridge disclose this limitation.
The NIST roadmap is general to smart manufacturing and does not provide a five-year adoption study for small U.S. lubricant formulators, so rho remains an appropriately labeled estimate.

The Moove filing calls the cited 1.3% forecast global lubricants market value growth at current retail prices, while the d5 bridge initially calls it real market growth; the record later acknowledges that constant-quality quantity is inferred. Correcting this wording or lowering d5 would not change the STRUCTURAL_OUT base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.
Global industry fragmentation and an issuer's operating model do not establish the eligible share of the 82 injected U.S. LMM-band firms; e is therefore judgmental, as disclosed.

The 0.60 retention estimate is not directly measured by the filing or a representative contract sample. The filing supports formulation, service, and supply-reliability differentiation, but not a numeric retained-benefit rate; the ESTIMATE label is honest.

EPA's used-oil guidance concerns used-oil handlers rather than directly proving that 95% of year-five lubricant quantity requires an accountable formulator. The o rationale is an openly disclosed estimate, and a correction within a plausible range would not change the base tier or action.

sources_audited.note: The BLS page is the cited May 2021 NAICS 324000 occupational roster and supports the broader-subsector occupation mix used as a disclosed proxy; it is not six-digit-specific.

The current BLS industry page reports the four quoted 2025 occupation counts: 18,720 refinery/pump operators, 5,230 mixing and blending operators, 4,980 production supervisors, and 3,290 heavy truck drivers.

The NIST publication page identifies smart-manufacturing applications and the cited data-management, heterogeneous-system integration, explainability, reliability, and safety challenges; its scope is general manufacturing rather than lubricant formulators.

The filing supports global fragmentation, formulation and technical-service complexity, supply reliability, and the quoted 1.2% manufacturer profit-pool forecast. It describes the 1.3% forecast as global market value growth at current retail prices, not real or constant-quality quantity growth, and it does not directly measure eligibility or retention.

Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; the record correctly treats intentions from a cross-industry, generally smaller population as a proxy rather than completed control transfers.

EPA supports physical handling, storage, analysis, recordkeeping, spill response, and recycling requirements for used-oil handlers, but it does not directly establish the year-five operator-required share for new-lubricant formulators.
